  1. 📉 Statistics & Modelling
  2. Time Series Analysis with Python

Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA

Python vs R Comparison

Author

G. I. Hansamal

Introduction

This document covers two foundational pillars of classical time series analysis — decomposition and ARIMA modelling — with side-by-side Python and R code throughout.

Environment note: Python chunks are executed live (via jupyter: python3). R equivalents are shown in fenced r blocks for reference. To run R, switch the YAML engine to knitr and rename {python} chunks to {r}.

Topics covered:

  1. Setup & data
  2. Visualisation
  3. Classical decomposition (additive & multiplicative)
  4. STL decomposition
  5. Stationarity testing (ADF & KPSS)
  6. ACF & PACF
  7. ARIMA — theory, manual fitting, diagnostics
  8. Auto-ARIMA
  9. SARIMA — seasonal extension
  10. Forecasting & model comparison

1. Setup & Data

Python

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.dates as mdates

from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import adfuller, kpss
from statsmodels.tsa.seasonal import seasonal_decompose, STL
from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA
from statsmodels.tsa.statespace.sarimax import SARIMAX
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf, plot_pacf
from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import acorr_ljungbox
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, mean_squared_error
from scipy.stats import probplot, norm

plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.dpi": 120,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.3,
})

print("Python ready.")

R Equivalent

# install.packages(c("forecast", "tseries", "ggplot2", "feasts", "tsibble"))
library(forecast)   # ARIMA, auto.arima, ETS
library(tseries)    # adf.test, kpss.test
library(ggplot2)
library(feasts)     # ACF, decomposition (tidyverts)
library(tsibble)

Simulated Dataset

We simulate 10 years of monthly retail sales with trend, seasonality, and noise.

np.random.seed(42)
n = 120
t = np.arange(n)

trend    = 200 + 2.5 * t
seasonal = 50 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * t / 12) + 20 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * t / 6)
noise    = np.random.normal(0, 15, n)
sales    = trend + seasonal + noise

dates = pd.date_range(start="2014-01-01", periods=n, freq="MS")
df = pd.DataFrame({"sales": sales}, index=dates)

# Train / test split — hold out last 12 months
train = df["sales"][:-12]
test  = df["sales"][-12:]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 4))
ax.plot(df.index, df["sales"], linewidth=1.5, color="steelblue")
ax.axvline(test.index[0], color="crimson", linestyle="--", label="Train / Test split")
ax.set_title("Simulated Monthly Retail Sales", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Date")
ax.set_ylabel("Sales (units)")
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.YearLocator())
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter("%Y"))
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(df.describe().round(2))

R Equivalent

set.seed(42)
n     <- 120
t_seq <- 0:(n - 1)

sales <- 200 + 2.5*t_seq +
         50*sin(2*pi*t_seq/12) + 20*sin(2*pi*t_seq/6) +
         rnorm(n, 0, 15)

# Base ts object — used by forecast package
ts_sales <- ts(sales, start = c(2014, 1), frequency = 12)

train_r <- window(ts_sales, end   = c(2022, 12))
test_r  <- window(ts_sales, start = c(2023,  1))

plot(ts_sales, main = "Simulated Monthly Retail Sales",
     ylab = "Sales", xlab = "Date", col = "steelblue")
abline(v = 2023, col = "crimson", lty = 2)

2. Visualisation

Rolling Statistics

Rolling mean and standard deviation reveal non-stationarity: a rising mean confirms a trend, growing spread suggests heteroscedasticity.

roll_mean = df["sales"].rolling(12).mean()
roll_std  = df["sales"].rolling(12).std()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(11, 6), sharex=True)

axes[0].plot(df.index, df["sales"],  alpha=0.5,  label="Observed",    color="steelblue")
axes[0].plot(df.index, roll_mean,    linewidth=2, label="12-mo Mean",  color="crimson")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Sales")
axes[0].legend()
axes[0].set_title("Rolling Statistics", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")

axes[1].plot(df.index, roll_std, color="darkorange", label="12-mo Std Dev")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Std Dev")
axes[1].legend()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

R Equivalent

library(zoo)
roll_mean <- rollmean(ts_sales, k = 12, fill = NA, align = "right")
roll_sd   <- rollapply(ts_sales, width = 12, FUN = sd, fill = NA, align = "right")

par(mfrow = c(2, 1), mar = c(3, 4, 2, 1))
plot(ts_sales, col = "steelblue", ylab = "Sales", main = "Rolling Statistics")
lines(roll_mean, col = "crimson", lwd = 2)
legend("topleft", legend = c("Observed", "12-mo Mean"),
       col = c("steelblue","crimson"), lty = 1)
plot(roll_sd, col = "darkorange", ylab = "Std Dev")

Seasonal Subseries Plot

ds = df.copy()
ds["year"]  = ds.index.year
ds["month"] = ds.index.month
month_labels = ["Jan","Feb","Mar","Apr","May","Jun",
                "Jul","Aug","Sep","Oct","Nov","Dec"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 4))
for year, grp in ds.groupby("year"):
    ax.plot(grp["month"], grp["sales"], alpha=0.45, marker="o",
            markersize=3, label=str(year))

monthly_means = ds.groupby("month")["sales"].mean()
ax.plot(monthly_means.index, monthly_means.values,
        color="black", linewidth=2.5, linestyle="--", label="Monthly mean")

ax.set_xticks(range(1, 13))
ax.set_xticklabels(month_labels)
ax.set_xlabel("Month")
ax.set_ylabel("Sales")
ax.set_title("Seasonal Plot by Year", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
ax.legend(ncol=6, fontsize=8)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

R Equivalent

# forecast package
seasonplot(ts_sales, year.labels = TRUE, main = "Seasonal Plot",
           col = rainbow(10), year.labels.left = TRUE)

# feasts / tidyverts
library(tsibble); library(feasts); library(dplyr); library(lubridate)
tsibble_sales <- data.frame(
  month = yearmonth(seq(as.Date("2014-01-01"), by="month", length.out=120)),
  sales = sales
) |> as_tsibble(index = month)

tsibble_sales |> gg_season(sales, labels = "both")

3. Classical Decomposition

Classical decomposition separates a series into Trend (T), Seasonal (S), and Remainder (R) components.

Model Formula When to use
Additive \(Y_t = T_t + S_t + R_t\) Seasonal swings are roughly constant
Multiplicative \(Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times R_t\) Seasonal swings grow with the level

The trend is estimated with a centred moving average (order = period), then seasonality is averaged across years, and the remainder is what’s left over.

3.1 Additive Decomposition

decomp_add = seasonal_decompose(df["sales"], model="additive", period=12)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 1, figsize=(11, 10), sharex=True)
parts = {
    "Observed" : (decomp_add.observed,  "steelblue"),
    "Trend"    : (decomp_add.trend,     "crimson"),
    "Seasonal" : (decomp_add.seasonal,  "darkorange"),
    "Residual" : (decomp_add.resid,     "purple"),
}
for ax, (label, (data, color)) in zip(axes, parts.items()):
    ax.plot(data, color=color, linewidth=1.2)
    ax.set_ylabel(label, fontsize=10)
    if label == "Residual":
        ax.axhline(0, linestyle="--", color="gray", alpha=0.5)

axes[0].set_title("Classical Additive Decomposition", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

R Equivalent

decomp_add <- decompose(ts_sales, type = "additive")
plot(decomp_add)

# Access components
decomp_add$trend
decomp_add$seasonal
decomp_add$random

3.2 Multiplicative Decomposition

decomp_mult = seasonal_decompose(df["sales"], model="multiplicative", period=12)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 1, figsize=(11, 10), sharex=True)
parts_m = {
    "Observed" : (decomp_mult.observed,  "steelblue"),
    "Trend"    : (decomp_mult.trend,     "crimson"),
    "Seasonal" : (decomp_mult.seasonal,  "darkorange"),
    "Residual" : (decomp_mult.resid,     "purple"),
}
for ax, (label, (data, color)) in zip(axes, parts_m.items()):
    ax.plot(data, color=color, linewidth=1.2)
    ax.set_ylabel(label, fontsize=10)
    if label == "Residual":
        ax.axhline(1, linestyle="--", color="gray", alpha=0.5)  # residual ~ 1 in multiplicative

axes[0].set_title("Classical Multiplicative Decomposition", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

R Equivalent

decomp_mult <- decompose(ts_sales, type = "multiplicative")
plot(decomp_mult)

3.3 STL Decomposition (Seasonal-Trend via Loess)

STL is more flexible than classical decomposition:

  • Handles changing seasonality over time
  • Robust to outliers (with robust=True)
  • Works for any seasonal period
  • Always additive (log-transform the series first for multiplicative behaviour)
stl = STL(df["sales"], period=12, robust=True)
stl_res = stl.fit()

fig = stl_res.plot()
fig.set_size_inches(11, 9)
fig.suptitle("STL Decomposition (Robust)", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", y=1.01)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Seasonal strength: 0 = no seasonality, 1 = perfectly seasonal
var_resid    = np.var(stl_res.resid)
var_seasonal = np.var(stl_res.seasonal + stl_res.resid)
strength = max(0, 1 - var_resid / var_seasonal)
print(f"Seasonal strength F_S = {strength:.4f}  (>0.64 = strong)")

R Equivalent

stl_r <- stl(ts_sales, s.window = "periodic", robust = TRUE)
plot(stl_r, main = "STL Decomposition (Robust)")

# Seasonal strength via feasts
tsibble_sales |> features(sales, feat_stl)

4. Stationarity Testing

ARIMA requires a stationary series — one whose mean, variance, and autocorrelation structure do not change over time. Two complementary tests are standard practice.

Test H₀ Reject if Conclusion when rejected
ADF Unit root (non-stationary) p < 0.05 Series is stationary
KPSS Stationary p < 0.05 Series is not stationary

Use both: if ADF rejects and KPSS does not, you have strong evidence of stationarity.

4.1 Augmented Dickey-Fuller

def adf_report(series, name):
    res = adfuller(series.dropna(), autolag="AIC")
    verdict = "STATIONARY ✓" if res[1] < 0.05 else "NON-STATIONARY ✗"
    print(f"\nADF — {name}")
    print(f"  Statistic : {res[0]:.4f}")
    print(f"  p-value   : {res[1]:.4f}  →  {verdict}")
    print(f"  Lags used : {res[2]}")
    for k, v in res[4].items():
        print(f"  Critical {k}: {v:.4f}")

adf_report(df["sales"],        "Original series")
adf_report(df["sales"].diff(), "First difference")

R Equivalent

library(tseries)
adf.test(ts_sales)          # original
adf.test(diff(ts_sales))    # first difference

# More control via urca
library(urca)
summary(ur.df(ts_sales, type = "drift", selectlags = "AIC"))

4.2 KPSS Test

def kpss_report(series, name):
    stat, pval, lags, crits = kpss(series.dropna(), regression="c", nlags="auto")
    verdict = "NON-STATIONARY ✗" if pval < 0.05 else "STATIONARY ✓"
    print(f"\nKPSS — {name}")
    print(f"  Statistic : {stat:.4f}")
    print(f"  p-value   : {pval:.4f}  →  {verdict}")
    print(f"  Lags used : {lags}")

kpss_report(df["sales"],        "Original series")
kpss_report(df["sales"].diff(), "First difference")

R Equivalent

kpss.test(ts_sales)
kpss.test(diff(ts_sales))

5. ACF & PACF

The ACF and PACF plots guide the selection of the AR order p and MA order q.

Pattern Implication
ACF cuts off at lag q, PACF tails off Pure MA(q)
PACF cuts off at lag p, ACF tails off Pure AR(p)
Both tail off gradually ARMA(p, q) — choose smallest model by AIC
Spike at lag m in ACF Seasonal component at period m

Always inspect ACF/PACF on the differenced (stationary) series.

sales_diff = df["sales"].diff().dropna()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))
plot_acf( sales_diff, lags=36, ax=axes[0], title="ACF — First Difference",  zero=False)
plot_pacf(sales_diff, lags=36, ax=axes[1], title="PACF — First Difference", zero=False, method="ywm")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

R Equivalent

sales_diff <- diff(ts_sales)
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
acf( sales_diff, lag.max = 36, main = "ACF — First Difference")
pacf(sales_diff, lag.max = 36, main = "PACF — First Difference")

# tidyverts
tsibble_sales |>
  mutate(d_sales = difference(sales)) |>
  ACF(d_sales, lag_max = 36) |> autoplot()

6. ARIMA Modelling

Theory

ARIMA(p, d, q) combines three components:

  • AR(p) — Autoregressive: \(Y_t\) depends on its own past p values
  • I(d) — Integrated: series is differenced d times to achieve stationarity
  • MA(q) — Moving Average: \(Y_t\) depends on past q forecast errors

\[ \phi(B)(1-B)^d Y_t = \theta(B)\varepsilon_t \]

where \(B\) is the backshift operator, \(\phi\) are AR coefficients, \(\theta\) are MA coefficients, and \(\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\).

Selection heuristic:

  1. Determine d from stationarity tests
  2. Read p from PACF (cuts off at lag p)
  3. Read q from ACF (cuts off at lag q)
  4. Compare candidate models by AIC / BIC

6.1 Manual Fitting

arima_model = ARIMA(train, order=(2, 1, 2))
arima_fit   = arima_model.fit()
print(arima_fit.summary())

R Equivalent

arima_r <- arima(train_r, order = c(2, 1, 2))
summary(arima_r)

# or with forecast package (adds tidy AIC/BIC)
arima_r2 <- Arima(train_r, order = c(2, 1, 2))
summary(arima_r2)

6.2 Forecasting with ARIMA

fc_obj  = arima_fit.get_forecast(steps=12)
fc_mean = fc_obj.predicted_mean
fc_ci   = fc_obj.conf_int(alpha=0.05)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5))
ax.plot(train.index[-36:], train[-36:], label="Training",  color="steelblue")
ax.plot(test.index,         test,        label="Actual",   color="black", linewidth=2)
ax.plot(fc_mean.index,      fc_mean,     label="Forecast", color="crimson",
        linestyle="--", linewidth=2)
ax.fill_between(fc_ci.index, fc_ci.iloc[:, 0], fc_ci.iloc[:, 1],
                alpha=0.2, color="crimson", label="95% CI")
ax.set_title("ARIMA(2,1,2) Forecast", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Date")
ax.set_ylabel("Sales")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

mae  = mean_absolute_error(test, fc_mean)
rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(test, fc_mean))
print(f"MAE  = {mae:.2f}")
print(f"RMSE = {rmse:.2f}")

R Equivalent

fc_arima_r <- forecast(arima_r2, h = 12)
autoplot(fc_arima_r) +
  autolayer(test_r, series = "Actual", colour = "black") +
  labs(title = "ARIMA(2,1,2) Forecast") +
  theme_minimal()

accuracy(fc_arima_r, test_r)

6.3 Residual Diagnostics

A well-fitted model should have residuals that are:

  • Centred on zero with no trend
  • Uncorrelated (white noise) — check with Ljung-Box
  • Approximately normally distributed
residuals = arima_fit.resid.dropna()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 8))

# Residuals over time
axes[0, 0].plot(residuals, color="steelblue", linewidth=0.8)
axes[0, 0].axhline(0, color="crimson", linestyle="--", alpha=0.6)
axes[0, 0].set_title("Residuals Over Time")

# Histogram + normal fit
axes[0, 1].hist(residuals, bins=25, edgecolor="white",
                color="steelblue", density=True)
xr = np.linspace(residuals.min(), residuals.max(), 200)
axes[0, 1].plot(xr, norm.pdf(xr, residuals.mean(), residuals.std()),
                color="crimson", lw=2, label="Normal")
axes[0, 1].set_title("Histogram of Residuals")
axes[0, 1].legend()

# ACF of residuals
plot_acf(residuals, lags=30, ax=axes[1, 0],
         title="ACF of Residuals", zero=False)

# Q-Q plot
probplot(residuals, plot=axes[1, 1])
axes[1, 1].set_title("Q-Q Plot")

plt.suptitle("ARIMA(2,1,2) Residual Diagnostics",
             fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", y=1.01)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Ljung-Box test
lb = acorr_ljungbox(residuals, lags=[10, 20], return_df=True)
print("\nLjung-Box Test (p > 0.05 = no autocorrelation ✓):")
print(lb.round(4))

R Equivalent

# One-line diagnostic plot + Ljung-Box
checkresiduals(arima_r2)

# Ljung-Box only
Box.test(residuals(arima_r2), lag = 20, type = "Ljung-Box")

6.4 Auto-ARIMA

Instead of reading ACF/PACF manually, auto-ARIMA searches over (p, d, q) combinations and selects by AIC.

try:
    from pmdarima import auto_arima
    auto_model = auto_arima(
        train,
        seasonal=False,
        d=None,                       # auto-detect d
        information_criterion="aic",
        stepwise=True,
        trace=True,
        error_action="ignore",
        suppress_warnings=True,
    )
    print(f"\nSelected order : {auto_model.order}")
    print(f"AIC            : {auto_model.aic():.2f}")
except ImportError:
    print("Install pmdarima:  pip install pmdarima")

R Equivalent

# auto.arima is built into the forecast package — no extra install
auto_r <- auto.arima(train_r, stepwise = FALSE, approximation = FALSE,
                     trace = TRUE)
summary(auto_r)
# Outputs selected (p,d,q), coefficients, AIC, BIC, RMSE

7. SARIMA — Seasonal ARIMA

ARIMA ignores seasonality. SARIMA adds a seasonal AR/I/MA structure at period m:

\[ \text{SARIMA}(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)_m \]

Parameter Meaning
P Seasonal AR order
D Seasonal differencing (usually 0 or 1)
Q Seasonal MA order
m Seasonal period (12 = monthly)

Seasonal spikes in the ACF at lags m, 2m, … suggest a seasonal MA term. Seasonal spikes in the PACF suggest a seasonal AR term.

7.1 Fitting SARIMA

sarima_model = SARIMAX(
    train,
    order=(1, 1, 1),
    seasonal_order=(1, 1, 1, 12),
    enforce_stationarity=False,
    enforce_invertibility=False,
)
sarima_fit = sarima_model.fit(disp=False)
print(sarima_fit.summary())

R Equivalent

sarima_r <- Arima(train_r,
                  order    = c(1, 1, 1),
                  seasonal = list(order = c(1, 1, 1), period = 12))
summary(sarima_r)

7.2 SARIMA Forecast

fc_s      = sarima_fit.get_forecast(steps=12)
fc_s_mean = fc_s.predicted_mean
fc_s_ci   = fc_s.conf_int(alpha=0.05)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5))
ax.plot(train.index[-36:], train[-36:], label="Training",  color="steelblue")
ax.plot(test.index,         test,        label="Actual",   color="black", linewidth=2)
ax.plot(fc_s_mean.index,    fc_s_mean,   label="Forecast", color="seagreen",
        linestyle="--", linewidth=2)
ax.fill_between(fc_s_ci.index, fc_s_ci.iloc[:, 0], fc_s_ci.iloc[:, 1],
                alpha=0.2, color="seagreen", label="95% CI")
ax.set_title("SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1)[12] Forecast", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Date")
ax.set_ylabel("Sales")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

mae_s  = mean_absolute_error(test, fc_s_mean)
rmse_s = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(test, fc_s_mean))
print(f"MAE  = {mae_s:.2f}")
print(f"RMSE = {rmse_s:.2f}")

R Equivalent

fc_sarima_r <- forecast(sarima_r, h = 12)
autoplot(fc_sarima_r) +
  autolayer(test_r, series = "Actual", colour = "black") +
  labs(title = "SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1)[12] Forecast") +
  theme_minimal()

accuracy(fc_sarima_r, test_r)

7.3 SARIMA Residual Diagnostics

res_s = sarima_fit.resid.dropna()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 8))

axes[0, 0].plot(res_s, color="steelblue", linewidth=0.8)
axes[0, 0].axhline(0, color="crimson", linestyle="--", alpha=0.6)
axes[0, 0].set_title("Residuals Over Time")

axes[0, 1].hist(res_s, bins=25, edgecolor="white",
                color="steelblue", density=True)
xr = np.linspace(res_s.min(), res_s.max(), 200)
axes[0, 1].plot(xr, norm.pdf(xr, res_s.mean(), res_s.std()),
                color="crimson", lw=2, label="Normal")
axes[0, 1].set_title("Histogram of Residuals")
axes[0, 1].legend()

plot_acf(res_s, lags=36, ax=axes[1, 0], title="ACF of Residuals", zero=False)
probplot(res_s, plot=axes[1, 1])
axes[1, 1].set_title("Q-Q Plot")

plt.suptitle("SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1)[12] Residual Diagnostics",
             fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", y=1.01)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

lb_s = acorr_ljungbox(res_s, lags=[12, 24], return_df=True)
print("\nLjung-Box Test:")
print(lb_s.round(4))

R Equivalent

checkresiduals(sarima_r)
Box.test(residuals(sarima_r), lag = 24, type = "Ljung-Box")

8. Model Comparison

def mape(y_true, y_pred):
    return np.mean(np.abs((y_true - y_pred) / y_true)) * 100

fc_arima_aligned  = arima_fit.get_forecast(steps=12).predicted_mean
fc_sarima_aligned = sarima_fit.get_forecast(steps=12).predicted_mean
fc_arima_aligned.index  = test.index
fc_sarima_aligned.index = test.index

rows = [
    {"Model": "ARIMA(2,1,2)",
     "MAE" : round(mean_absolute_error(test, fc_arima_aligned),          2),
     "RMSE": round(np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(test, fc_arima_aligned)),  2),
     "MAPE": round(mape(test.values, fc_arima_aligned.values),           2)},
    {"Model": "SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1)[12]",
     "MAE" : round(mean_absolute_error(test, fc_sarima_aligned),         2),
     "RMSE": round(np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(test, fc_sarima_aligned)), 2),
     "MAPE": round(mape(test.values, fc_sarima_aligned.values),          2)},
]
print(pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index("Model").to_string())

# Plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))
ax.plot(train.index[-24:], train[-24:],      label="Training",   color="steelblue", lw=1.5)
ax.plot(test.index,         test,             label="Actual",     color="black",     lw=2.5)
ax.plot(fc_arima_aligned.index,  fc_arima_aligned,
        label="ARIMA(2,1,2)",             color="crimson",   lw=2, linestyle="--")
ax.plot(fc_sarima_aligned.index, fc_sarima_aligned,
        label="SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1)[12]", color="seagreen",  lw=2, linestyle="--")
ax.set_title("ARIMA vs SARIMA Forecast Comparison", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Date")
ax.set_ylabel("Sales")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

R Equivalent

accuracy(fc_arima_r,  test_r)
accuracy(fc_sarima_r, test_r)

# Combined plot
autoplot(fc_sarima_r) +
  autolayer(fc_arima_r,  series = "ARIMA",  PI = FALSE) +
  autolayer(test_r,      series = "Actual", colour = "black") +
  labs(title = "ARIMA vs SARIMA") +
  theme_minimal()

9. Python vs R: Quick Reference

Task Python R
Time series object pd.Series with DatetimeIndex ts(), tsibble
Classical decompose seasonal_decompose() (statsmodels) decompose() (stats)
STL decompose STL() (statsmodels) stl() (stats)
ADF test adfuller() (statsmodels) adf.test() (tseries)
KPSS test kpss() (statsmodels) kpss.test() (tseries)
ACF / PACF plot_acf/pacf() (statsmodels) acf/pacf() or ACF() (feasts)
Manual ARIMA ARIMA() (statsmodels) Arima() (forecast)
Auto-ARIMA auto_arima() (pmdarima — extra install) auto.arima() (forecast — built-in)
SARIMA SARIMAX() (statsmodels) Arima(seasonal=…) (forecast)
Residual diagnostics Manual (acorr_ljungbox + plots) checkresiduals() — one line
Forecast object .get_forecast() + .conf_int() forecast() + autoplot()

Key differences:

  • R’s forecast package provides auto.arima() and checkresiduals() out of the box; Python needs pmdarima for the equivalent auto-selection.
  • Python gives finer programmatic control and integrates naturally with ML pipelines.
  • R’s autoplot() produces publication-quality forecast plots with one line.

10. Workflow Checklist

Use this checklist for every new time series project:

  1. Plot the raw series — look for trend, seasonality, structural breaks, outliers
  2. Run rolling mean/std to check for non-stationarity
  3. Decompose (STL is almost always the better choice over classical)
  4. Run ADF + KPSS on the original and differenced series; determine d
  5. Plot ACF / PACF on the stationary series; read off candidate p and q
  6. Fit ARIMA and SARIMA; compare AIC/BIC
  7. Diagnose residuals — Ljung-Box, Q-Q plot, histogram
  8. Forecast and evaluate on a held-out test set (MAE, RMSE, MAPE)
  9. Use auto-ARIMA as a sanity check against your manual model

Session Info

import sys, statsmodels, sklearn, numpy, pandas, matplotlib
print(f"Python      : {sys.version.split()[0]}")
print(f"pandas      : {pandas.__version__}")
print(f"numpy       : {numpy.__version__}")
print(f"statsmodels : {statsmodels.__version__}")
print(f"scikit-learn: {sklearn.__version__}")
print(f"matplotlib  : {matplotlib.__version__}")
